era_context: The Roman Empire under Constantius II continues to consolidate power after the civil war with Magnentius. The usurper's final defeat is imminent. Julian is newly appointed as Caesar and beginning his governorship of Gaul. The Sassanid Persian Empire under Shapur II remains a significant threat on the eastern frontier. Christianity is increasingly dominant, with theological disputes over Arianism shaping imperial policy.
geographic_coverage_gaps: East Asia (Chinese sources may be underrepresented)

events.title: Battle of Mons Seleucus: Constantius II defeats Magnentius
events.description: In a decisive battle near the Seleucus mountains (location debated, possibly near the Doubs River in eastern Gaul), Constantius II's forces decisively defeat the usurper Magnentius. This victory effectively ends the decade-long civil war that had divided the Western Roman Empire. Magnentius is forced to flee westward and will commit suicide within the year. The battle is significant not only for its military outcome but for demonstrating Constantius II's military competence and the loyalty of his generals. The victory allows Constantius to consolidate his authority and turn his attention to other threats.
events.certainty_note: The battle is well-attested in multiple sources, though the exact location and date (353 vs. 354) are debated. Most modern scholars place it in 353, but some sources date it to 354.
cross_references: Part of broader civil war (350–353 CE)

events.title: Augustine of Hippo born
events.description: Augustine (Aurelius Augustinus) is born in Thagaste (modern Souk Ahras, Algeria) in North Africa. He will become one of the most influential Christian theologians and philosophers in history, shaping Western Christianity for over a millennium. At this time, Augustine is not yet Christian—he will undergo conversion in 386 CE. His early life is marked by classical education and philosophical inquiry. His later works, including the Confessions and City of God, will profoundly influence Christian theology, philosophy, and political thought.
events.certainty_note: Augustine's birth year is well-established from his own writings and later biographical sources. The date 354 CE is universally accepted by modern scholarship.

events.title: Shapur II of Sassanid Persia campaigns on eastern frontier
events.description: Shapur II, the Sassanid Persian king, continues his military campaigns against Roman-aligned territories and tribes on the eastern frontier. These campaigns are part of a broader pattern of Sassanid expansion and consolidation of control over Mesopotamia and the Caucasus region. Shapur II is one of the most militarily active Sassanid rulers, and his campaigns pose a significant threat to Roman interests in the East. The exact details of campaigns in 354 CE are sparse, but Shapur II's reign (309–379 CE) is marked by continuous military activity.

sources.name: Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings (later Islamic source)

events.certainty_note: Shapur II's reign is well-documented, but specific campaigns in 354 CE are not clearly dated in surviving sources. This entry reflects the broader pattern of Sassanid military activity during this period.
cross_references: Part of broader Roman-Sassanid conflict (3rd–7th centuries CE)
Contemporary with Constantius II's consolidation in the West

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Battle of Mons Seleucus is disputed. Some sources place it in 353 CE, others in 354 CE. Modern scholarship is divided, with most scholars favoring 353 CE, but the exact date remains uncertain. Additionally, the location of the battle is debated—some sources suggest it occurred near the Rhine, others near the Doubs or other locations in eastern Gaul. The significance of the battle as a 'decisive' victory is also somewhat overstated in later sources; the civil war continued for another year after this battle.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 354 CE is moderately rich for the Roman Empire but heavily skewed toward the Western Empire and Christian sources. Ammianus Marcellinus remains the most detailed secular source, but he was not born until 330 CE and is writing retrospectively. Ecclesiastical sources focus on theological matters and the lives of Christian figures (like Augustine's birth) rather than political and military events. Sources from the Sassanid Empire are limited and often filtered through later Islamic historiography. The record for regions outside the Mediterranean and Near East is virtually absent.

graph_edges.note: The Battle of Mons Seleucus leads directly to Magnentius's final defeat and suicide in 355
Augustine's birth in 354 precedes his conversion to Christianity in 386
Shapur II's campaigns are part of the broader Roman-Sassanid conflict that culminates in Julian's Persian campaign in 363
